question: The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their:
options: Atomic mass
Atomic number
Mass number
Number of neutrons
explanation: The Modern Periodic Law (Moseley, 1913) states: 'The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers (proton numbers).' This corrected Mendeleev's original law which used atomic mass. Moseley's X-ray frequency experiments showed atomic number (not mass) is the fundamental property determining element identity. This resolved anomalies in Mendeleev's table (like Ar/K and Co/Ni inversions where heavier element preceded lighter by atomic mass but followed by atomic number).

question: Mendeleev's periodic table arranged elements by increasing atomic mass. Which of the following anomalies in his table was corrected by the modern periodic law?
options: Placement of noble gases
Reversal of Ar (39.9) and K (39.1) — Ar appearing before K despite having greater mass
The existence of isotopes
Lanthanide contraction
explanation: In Mendeleev's mass-based table, Argon (Ar, mass 39.9) would appear AFTER Potassium (K, mass 39.1), yet Ar has chemical properties of a noble gas and K has properties of an alkali metal — placing them in wrong groups by mass. By atomic number: Ar (Z=18) correctly precedes K (Z=19). Similar inversions occurred for Co (58.9) before Ni (58.7) and Te (127.6) before I (126.9). The modern periodic law resolved all these inversions. Noble gases weren't even known to Mendeleev (discovered later); isotopes came after the modern law.

question: The periodic table has how many periods and groups in total?
options: 6 periods, 16 groups
7 periods, 18 groups
8 periods, 18 groups
7 periods, 16 groups
explanation: The modern long-form periodic table has 7 periods (horizontal rows) and 18 groups (vertical columns) using IUPAC numbering (1–18). Period 1 has 2 elements; Periods 2 and 3 have 8 elements each; Periods 4 and 5 have 18 elements each; Period 6 has 32 elements (including lanthanides); Period 7 has 32 elements (including actinides). The 18-group system replaced the old A/B subgroup notation. The periodic table currently contains 118 confirmed elements.

question: Which block of the periodic table contains elements where the last electron enters an f orbital?
options: s-block
p-block
d-block
f-block
explanation: Block classification based on the subshell being filled by the last electron: s-block (groups 1-2): last electron in s orbital; p-block (groups 13-18): last electron in p orbital; d-block (groups 3-12): last electron in d orbital (transition metals); f-block (lanthanides/actinides): last electron in f orbital. Lanthanides: 4f is being filled (La to Lu); Actinides: 5f is being filled (Ac to Lr). The f-block is placed separately below the main table for compactness.

question: Atomic radius generally decreases across a period from left to right because:
options: Electrons are added to inner shells, reducing size
Nuclear charge increases while electrons are added to the same shell, increasing Zeff
The number of electron shells increases across a period
Electron-electron repulsion decreases across a period
explanation: Across a period (left to right): nuclear charge (Z) increases by 1 for each element, but electrons are added to the SAME outermost shell (same n). The shielding by electrons in the same shell is poor (they don't effectively shield each other). So effective nuclear charge (Zeff = Z − σ) increases across a period, pulling the outer electrons closer to the nucleus → decreased atomic radius. Going down a group: new electron shells are added, increasing atomic radius despite higher Z. This is the primary explanation for the trend in atomic radii.

question: The first ionization energy trend across Period 3 shows two exceptions: Mg→Al and P→S. These exceptions occur because:
options: Al and S have higher electron affinity than expected
Al loses an electron from a higher energy 3p orbital; S has a paired electron in 3p that is easier to remove due to repulsion
Mg and P have extra stability due to filled and half-filled subshells respectively
The nuclear charge of Al and S is unexpectedly low
explanation: IE₁ generally increases across Period 3. Two exceptions: (1) Mg (IE = 738 kJ/mol) > Al (IE = 577 kJ/mol): Mg has a completely filled 3s² (extra stability of full subshell); Al's last electron is in the higher-energy 3p (less stable, easier to remove). (2) P (IE = 1012 kJ/mol) > S (IE = 1000 kJ/mol): P has half-filled 3p³ (3 unpaired electrons, maximum exchange energy, extra stability); S has one paired electron in 3p⁴ which experiences extra repulsion from its orbital partner, making it slightly easier to remove. Both exceptions demonstrate extra stability of filled/half-filled subshells.

question: Which of the following has the largest ionic radius?
options: Na⁺
Mg²⁺
Al³⁺
O²⁻
explanation: All options with Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺ are isoelectronic with 10 electrons (neon configuration). In isoelectronic ions, the more protons, the smaller the radius (more nuclear pull on same electrons). Na⁺ (Z=11) > Mg²⁺ (Z=12) > Al³⁺ (Z=13) in size. But O²⁻ has 8 protons and 10 electrons — fewer protons than Na⁺ means the nuclear pull is weakest for O²⁻, giving it the largest radius (~140 pm). The order: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < O²⁻. Anions are always larger than their parent atoms; highly charged cations are very small.

question: Electronegativity is defined as:
options: The energy released when an electron is added to an isolated gaseous atom
The tendency of a bonded atom to attract the shared pair of electrons toward itself
The energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom
The ability of an atom to lose electrons in a chemical reaction
explanation: Electronegativity (Pauling scale) is the tendency of an atom in a covalent bond to attract the shared electron pair toward itself. It is not a directly measurable quantity for isolated atoms — it only has meaning in the context of a chemical bond. Electron affinity (option A) is the energy released when an isolated gaseous atom gains an electron. Ionization energy (option C) measures ease of electron removal. Electropositivity (option D) is the tendency to lose electrons. Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element (4.0 on Pauling scale); Cesium (Cs) is the least electronegative.

question: The electron affinity of fluorine is LESS than that of chlorine, even though fluorine is more electronegative. The reason is:
options: Fluorine has fewer protons than chlorine
The small size of fluorine leads to strong electron-electron repulsions in the compact 2p subshell, reducing the energy released on adding an electron
Fluorine already has 7 valence electrons and resists gaining more
Chlorine is a gas while fluorine is more reactive
explanation: Expected trend: electron affinity increases across a period and UP a group (more electronegative elements should have higher EA). But F (EA = 328 kJ/mol) < Cl (EA = 349 kJ/mol). Reason: F has a very small atomic radius — adding an electron to the small, compact 2p subshell results in significant inter-electron repulsion, partially offsetting the energy released. The 2p subshell of F is already quite crowded. Cl is larger (3p subshell), so the added electron faces less repulsion. Similar anomaly exists: O has lower EA than S for the same reason (small size, 2p repulsion).

question: The diagonal relationship in the periodic table is observed between:
options: Adjacent elements in the same period
Elements in the same group but different periods
An element and the element diagonally below and to the right of it in the periodic table
Transition metals and their neighboring elements
explanation: Diagonal relationship: elements in Period 2 show similar properties to elements diagonally placed (one period lower, one group to the right) in Period 3. Examples: Li ~ Mg (both form stable carbonates, similar ionic radii), Be ~ Al (both amphoteric, similar charge/size ratio), B ~ Si (both semiconductors, similar covalent character), Na ~ Ca (to some extent). This occurs because the two effects — increasing period (larger atoms) and increasing group (more charge) — partially cancel, giving similar charge density (charge/radius ratio) and similar electronegativity.

question: Which of the following correctly arranges the elements in decreasing order of first ionization energy?
options: F > O > N > C
N > O > F > C
F > N > O > C
O > N > F > C
explanation: General trend: IE increases left to right across a period. But N > O due to the extra stability of half-filled N (2p³ configuration — maximum exchange energy). So the order in Period 2 is: F > N > O > C. Values: F ≈ 1681, N ≈ 1402, O ≈ 1314, C ≈ 1086 kJ/mol. The N > O reversal is a classic NEET question — P > S in Period 3 is the equivalent. The full Period 2 trend: He > Ne > F > N > O > Be > C > B > Mg... wait, within period 2 non-metals: F(1681) > N(1402) > O(1314) > C(1086). Option C matches.

question: The element with the highest electronegativity on the Pauling scale is:
options: Oxygen (O)
Nitrogen (N)
Chlorine (Cl)
Fluorine (F)
explanation: Fluorine (F) has the highest electronegativity of all elements: 4.0 on the Pauling scale. This is because F has the highest effective nuclear charge relative to its atomic size in the entire periodic table (it's in period 2, group 17 — top right of p-block). The electronegativity values (Pauling): F=4.0, O=3.5, N=3.0, Cl=3.0, Br=2.8. The extreme electronegativity of F explains: HF is a weak acid (strong H-F bond), F₂ is highly oxidizing, and HF forms strong hydrogen bonds making it a liquid at room temperature despite small molecular mass.

question: Metallic character of elements:
options: Increases across a period and down a group
Decreases across a period but increases down a group
Increases across a period and up a group
Decreases in both directions
explanation: Metallic character (tendency to lose electrons, form cations, show electropositive behavior) decreases across a period (as nuclear charge and electronegativity increase) but increases down a group (as atomic radius increases, outer electrons become easier to remove — ionization energy decreases). Therefore: Na is more metallic than Mg (same period); Cs is more metallic than Na (same group). The most metallic element is Francium (Fr, group 1, period 7) — though Cesium (Cs) is most commonly cited as it's the most metallic stable element. The least metallic non-radioactive element is Fluorine.

question:
Assertion (A): The second ionization energy of an element is always greater than its first ionization energy.
Reason (R): After removing the first electron, the remaining electrons are held more tightly by the now-greater effective nuclear charge per electron.
options: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A
Both A and R are true, but R does not explain A
A is true, R is false
Both A and R are false
explanation: Assertion A is TRUE: IE₂ > IE₁ always, because after removing one electron, the cation (with n−1 electrons) has the same nuclear charge but fewer electrons, so each remaining electron experiences more nuclear pull (higher Zeff). The ion is smaller and electrons are harder to remove. Reason R is TRUE and correctly explains A: removing one electron from n electrons leaves (n−1) electrons with the same Z, so Zeff per electron = Z/(n−1) > Z/n. The dramatic jump in ionization energies (e.g., Na: IE₁=496, IE₂=4563 kJ/mol) occurs when a noble-gas core electron is removed.

question: The trend in ionic radii across an isoelectronic series (e.g., N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺) is:
options: Ionic radius increases with increasing nuclear charge
Ionic radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge
Ionic radius remains constant since all have the same electrons
Ionic radius is unpredictable for isoelectronic species
explanation: In an isoelectronic series, all species have the same number of electrons (e.g., all have 10 electrons in this series). The only variable is nuclear charge (Z). Greater Z → stronger pull on the same 10 electrons → smaller ionic radius. Radii: N³⁻ (Z=7) > O²⁻ (Z=8) > F⁻ (Z=9) > Na⁺ (Z=11) > Mg²⁺ (Z=12). Values approx: N³⁻ (146 pm) > O²⁻ (140 pm) > F⁻ (133 pm) > Na⁺ (102 pm) > Mg²⁺ (72 pm). This trend is a direct consequence of increased nuclear attraction compressing the same electron cloud.

question: The lanthanide contraction refers to:
options: The decrease in atomic radius as we go down the lanthanide series
The unusually small increase in atomic radius from the 4d to 5d transition series due to poor shielding by 4f electrons
The contraction of the f subshell during lanthanide filling
The reduction in ionization energy across the lanthanides
explanation: Lanthanide contraction: as the 4f subshell fills across lanthanides (La to Lu), the 4f electrons shield outer electrons POORLY (because f orbitals are diffuse and have low penetration). This causes a steady contraction in atomic/ionic radii across the lanthanides. Consequence: the 5d transition series elements (Hf, Ta, W...) have almost identical atomic radii to their 4d counterparts (Zr, Nb, Mo...), despite being one period lower. This makes Zr/Hf, Nb/Ta, Mo/W chemical twins — extremely difficult to separate industrially. It also explains why gold (Au) is dense and has unique optical properties.

question: Which of the following elements is the best oxidizing agent?
options: Na
Mg
F₂
Cl₂
explanation: An oxidizing agent gains electrons (is reduced). The best oxidizing agent is the one most likely to gain electrons — the element with the highest electronegativity and electron affinity. Fluorine (F₂) has the highest reduction potential (+2.87 V) of any element — it oxidizes virtually every other substance, including water. Cl₂ is also a good oxidizing agent (+1.36 V) but weaker than F₂. Na and Mg are metals — they are REDUCING agents (electron donors), not oxidizing agents. In oxidizing power: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂ (down halogen group, oxidizing power decreases).

question: The element at the intersection of Period 3 and Group 16 is sulfur (S). Which property correctly identifies S as a non-metal?
options: It has a high melting point above 1000°C
It forms a basic oxide (SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄)
It is a poor conductor of electricity and forms acidic oxides
It forms cations by losing electrons in reactions
explanation: Non-metals are characterized by: poor electrical conductivity (sulfur is an insulator), formation of acidic oxides (SO₂, SO₃ dissolve in water to form H₂SO₃, H₂SO₄), negative oxidation states (gains electrons), high electronegativities, and brittle solid forms. Sulfur has melting point 115°C (low, not high). Its oxides (SO₂, SO₃) are ACIDIC (dissolve to form acids), not basic. Non-metals form anions (S²⁻), not cations. The combination of poor conductivity and acidic oxide formation definitively identifies S as a non-metal.

question: Atomic radius is difficult to define and measure precisely because:
options: Atoms are too small to observe directly
The boundary of the electron cloud is not sharply defined; atoms have no definite 'edge'
Different isotopes of the same element have different radii
Atoms change size during chemical reactions
explanation: The atomic radius cannot be precisely defined because electrons are described by probability distributions (orbitals) with no hard boundary — there is always some finite probability of finding an electron far from the nucleus. Therefore, 'atomic radius' is defined operationally: covalent radius (half the distance between two bonded identical atoms), van der Waals radius (half the distance between non-bonded atoms in contact), or metallic radius (half the interatomic distance in a metal crystal). Different definitions give different values. This is why atomic radii are 'estimated' or 'effective' values, not absolute physical boundaries.

question: The oxide of which element in Period 3 shows amphoteric behavior?
options: Na₂O
MgO
Al₂O₃
SiO₂
explanation: Across Period 3: oxides change from strongly basic (Na₂O, MgO) → amphoteric (Al₂O₃) → acidic (SiO₂, P₂O₅, SO₃, Cl₂O₇). Al₂O₃ is amphoteric — it reacts with both acids and bases: Al₂O₃ + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂O (acidic conditions), Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH + 3H₂O → 2Na[Al(OH)₄] (basic conditions). This amphoteric character of Al₂O₃ is why aluminum dissolves in both HCl and NaOH. Other amphoteric oxides: ZnO, PbO, Cr₂O₃. Al is on the metalloid boundary, explaining its intermediate oxide character.

question:
Match the following: 
1. Group 1 elements — P. 2 valence electrons
2. Group 2 elements — Q. 7 valence electrons
3. Group 17 elements — R. 6 valence electrons
4. Group 16 elements — S. 1 valence electron
The correct matching is:
explanation: Group number (for main group elements) = number of valence electrons (for groups 1-2 and 13-18 in the modern system). Group 1 (alkali metals like Li, Na, K): 1 valence electron = S. Group 2 (alkaline earth metals like Be, Mg, Ca): 2 valence electrons = P. Group 17 (halogens like F, Cl, Br): 7 valence electrons = Q. Group 16 (chalcogens like O, S, Se): 6 valence electrons = R. Matching: 1-S, 2-P, 3-Q, 4-R = Option A. This basic relationship (group number = valence electrons for main group) is fundamental for predicting reactivity and compound formation.

question: The first ionization energy of noble gases is generally the highest in their respective periods. This is because:
options: Noble gases have the largest atomic size in their period
Noble gases have completely filled valence shells, giving maximum stability
Noble gases have the most protons in their period
Noble gases do not form any chemical bonds
explanation: Noble gases have completely filled valence shells (s² p⁶ configuration, except He with 1s²), which represents maximum electronic stability (lowest energy for that electron configuration). Any disruption of this stable configuration — including removing an electron — requires a large amount of energy. They also have the highest Zeff in their period (highest nuclear charge with same number of shells), and the filled shell provides no additional shielding beyond their period's starting point. Note: they DO form some compounds (XeF₂, XeF₄, etc.), so option D is incorrect. The full valence shell is the primary reason.

question: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of atomic radius?
options: Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs
Cs < Rb < K < Na < Li
Na < Li < K < Rb < Cs
Li > Na > K > Cs > Rb
explanation: Going down Group 1 (alkali metals), atomic radius increases because each successive element adds a new electron shell: Li (n=2, 152 pm) < Na (n=3, 186 pm) < K (n=4, 227 pm) < Rb (n=5, 248 pm) < Cs (n=6, 265 pm). As n increases, the outermost electrons are further from the nucleus. The increasing nuclear charge (higher Z) is more than offset by the shielding of inner shells. This increasing radius trend explains the decreasing ionization energy and increasing reactivity of alkali metals down the group.

question: The negative electron gain enthalpy (electron affinity) of halogens is in the order:
options: F > Cl > Br > I
Cl > F > Br > I
F > Br > Cl > I
I > Br > Cl > F
explanation: Electron affinity (magnitude) order for halogens: Cl (349 kJ/mol) > F (328 kJ/mol) > Br (325 kJ/mol) > I (295 kJ/mol). The anomaly: Cl > F despite F being more electronegative. Reason: F has a very small, compact 2p subshell — adding an electron causes significant inter-electron repulsion (small orbital, crowded). Cl has a larger 3p subshell — the added electron faces less repulsion. Down the group (Cl → Br → I), the increasing size dilutes the attraction, reducing EA. This same pattern explains why F₂ has a weaker bond than Cl₂ (lone pair repulsions in small F).

question: In Period 4, the transition metals (Sc to Zn) show less variation in atomic radius than main group elements. This is because:
options: Transition metals have similar electronic configurations
Electrons are added to the inner 3d subshell, providing additional shielding that partially offsets the increasing nuclear charge
Transition metals all have the same atomic mass
The 4s electrons are removed before 3d during ionization
explanation: Across the d-block (Sc to Zn), electrons fill the 3d subshell (inner subshell), NOT the outermost 4s. The 3d electrons increase nuclear charge but ALSO provide additional shielding for the 4s electrons, so Zeff experienced by the 4s electrons changes relatively little. This contrasts with s and p blocks where the added electrons go into the same valence shell with poor inter-electron shielding. Result: atomic radii vary only from ~162 pm (Sc) to ~125 pm (Zn), much less than the p-block variation (~114 pm for C to ~209 pm for K across the same period).

question: Which of the following oxides is the most basic?
options: Al₂O₃
Na₂O
P₂O₅
Cl₂O₇
explanation: Oxide basicity increases with metallic character (ability to donate electrons to form OH⁻). Going left to right across Period 3: Na₂O (strongly basic) > MgO (basic) > Al₂O₃ (amphoteric) > SiO₂ (weakly acidic) > P₂O₅ (acidic) > SO₃ (strongly acidic) > Cl₂O₇ (most acidic). Na₂O is the most basic because Na is the most electropositive/metallic element in Period 3. It reacts vigorously with water: Na₂O + H₂O → 2NaOH. Cl₂O₇ is the most acidic (Cl is most electronegative in Period 3): Cl₂O₇ + H₂O → 2HClO₄ (perchloric acid, the strongest common acid).

question:
Assertion (A): The atomic radius of Ga (Z=31) is slightly smaller than that of Al (Z=13), despite Ga being in the period below Al.
Reason (R): The d-electrons filled across the 4th period (3d transition metals) provide poor shielding, increasing Zeff for Ga's outer electrons more than expected.
options: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A
A is true but R is false
A is false, R is true
Both A and R are false
explanation: A is TRUE: Al radius ≈ 143 pm, Ga radius ≈ 136 pm — Ga is slightly SMALLER than Al despite being one period below. This is the 'd-block contraction' effect. R is TRUE: Before Ga (Z=31), the 10 d-electrons (3d¹⁰) in Zn-Ga transition are filled. These 3d electrons shield the outer 4p electron of Ga poorly (d electrons have high angular momentum and penetrate the inner core less effectively), causing a higher-than-expected Zeff for Ga's valence electrons, making Ga smaller than anticipated. This same effect causes Ge to behave more like Si in some properties.

question: The most electronegative element has which set of properties?
options: Low ionization energy, large atomic radius, low electron affinity
High ionization energy, small atomic radius, high electron affinity
Low ionization energy, small atomic radius, high electron affinity
High ionization energy, large atomic radius, low electron affinity
explanation: Electronegativity correlates with the ability to attract bonding electrons toward an atom. An electronegative atom should: (1) have HIGH ionization energy (tightly holds its own electrons), (2) have SMALL atomic radius (electrons are close to nucleus, attractive force on bond pairs is large), (3) have HIGH electron affinity (readily accepts additional electrons). Fluorine exemplifies all three: IE = 1681 kJ/mol (high), radius = 64 pm (very small), EA = 328 kJ/mol (high). Low ionization energy and large radius describe metals — the opposite of electronegativity.

question: The hydrogen resembles both alkali metals (Group 1) and halogens (Group 17). Which property does it share with halogens?
options: It forms M⁺ ions like alkali metals
It can gain one electron to form H⁻ (hydride ion), like halogens gain electrons to form X⁻
It has valence electron configuration ns¹ like alkali metals
It reacts with water to form hydroxides
explanation: Hydrogen's dual character: Like Group 1 (alkali metals): has 1 valence electron (1s¹), forms H⁺, reacts with non-metals. Like Group 17 (halogens): can accept one electron to form H⁻ (hydride, like F⁻), non-metallic character, exists as diatomic H₂. The formation of ionic hydrides (e.g., NaH → Na⁺ + H⁻) shows H behaving like a halogen. This dual nature is why H is placed uniquely in the periodic table (sometimes Group 1, sometimes Group 17, sometimes given a separate position). Option B correctly identifies the halogen-like behavior.

question: In which period and group would an element with the electronic configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d³ 6s² be placed?
options: Period 5, Group 5
Period 6, Group 5
Period 6, Group 3
Period 7, Group 5
explanation: [Xe] = complete xenon core (54 electrons). 4f¹⁴ 5d³ 6s²: the highest principal quantum number is 6 (from 6s²), so it's in Period 6. The last electrons enter the 5d subshell, so it's a d-block element. For d-block, Group = (number of s electrons + number of d electrons in valence shell) = 2 + 3 = 5 → Group 5. This element is Tantalum (Ta, Z=73). In Group 5 Period 6: V (Period 4), Nb (Period 5), Ta (Period 6). Ta is used in electronics (tantalum capacitors) and surgical implants due to its biocompatibility.

question: Which of the following is true about the size of atoms and ions?
options: Cations are always larger than their parent atoms
Anions are smaller than their parent atoms
A cation is smaller than its parent atom; an anion is larger than its parent atom
The size of an ion is independent of charge
explanation: When an atom LOSES electrons to form a cation: fewer electrons, same nuclear charge → electrons are pulled closer → cation is SMALLER than parent atom. Example: Na (186 pm) → Na⁺ (102 pm). When an atom GAINS electrons to form an anion: more electrons, same nuclear charge → electrons spread out due to increased repulsion → anion is LARGER than parent atom. Example: Cl (99 pm) → Cl⁻ (181 pm). The ionic radius trend is critically important for understanding crystal structures, lattice energies, and chemical reactivity.

question: Period 2 elements show anomalous behavior compared to their heavier congeners in the same group because of:
options: Their higher nuclear charge
Their small size, high electronegativity, and unavailability of d orbitals for bonding
Their lower ionization energy
The presence of f electrons in Period 2
explanation: Period 2 elements (Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F) show distinct anomalies due to three factors: (1) Small atomic size — leads to high charge density and stronger bonds; (2) High electronegativity — especially for N, O, F; (3) No available d orbitals in the 2nd shell (n=2 has only s and p) — cannot expand octet or form hybrid orbitals involving d. Examples: N cannot form pentavalent compounds (unlike P which uses d orbitals); BeCl₂ has a different structure than MgCl₂; C forms π bonds easily (small size, good p orbital overlap) while Si forms weak π bonds. These are the 'unique properties of first member of a group.'

question: The element with atomic number 118 (Oganesson) belongs to Group 18. Based on periodic trends, which of the following properties is MOST expected for Oganesson?
options: It would be highly reactive, forming stable compounds with fluorine
It would be a solid at room temperature with some metallic character due to relativistic effects
It would be an ideal gas with no tendency to form compounds
It would have the highest ionization energy of all known elements
explanation: Oganesson (Og, Z=118) is a noble gas (Group 18, Period 7) but is unique due to strong RELATIVISTIC EFFECTS at very high Z. Relativistic contraction of s and p electrons is so significant that Og is predicted to be a solid at room temperature (unlike all lighter noble gases which are gases). Its 6p electrons are so diffuse (relativistic expansion) that it might form compounds. Its ionization energy would be much LOWER than expected for a noble gas (relativistic effects reduce IE). Theoretical calculations suggest it may show some chemical reactivity. This is an example of how periodic trends may break down for superheavy elements.

question: In the third period, the chloride that hydrolyzes most readily with water is:
options: NaCl
MgCl₂
AlCl₃
SiCl₄
explanation: Hydrolysis of chlorides across Period 3: NaCl — no hydrolysis (ionic compound, Na⁺ has low charge density). MgCl₂ — slight hydrolysis (Mg²⁺ hydrolyzes slightly in solution). AlCl₃ — more readily hydrolyzes (Al³⁺ has high charge density: 3+/small radius). SiCl₄ — reacts vigorously and completely with water: SiCl₄ + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 4HCl. PCl₅, PCl₃, and SCl₂ also hydrolyze. The trend: hydrolysis increases with increasing polarizing power (charge/radius ratio) and increasing covalent character (non-metals). SiCl₄ is a covalent chloride — Si has empty d orbitals that water's oxygen can coordinate to, facilitating hydrolysis. PCl₃ and PCl₅ also hydrolyze readily.

question: The nuclear charge is the same for all isotopes of an element, yet the atomic radius can vary slightly. This is because:
options: Isotopes have different electron configurations
More neutrons in the nucleus increase nuclear volume, slightly increasing atomic radius (nuclear volume effect)
The electron cloud expands due to neutron-electron interactions
Isotopes have different ionization energies that affect electron distance
explanation: Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons (Z) and electrons, so their electronic structure and chemical properties are virtually identical. However, different isotopes have different numbers of neutrons, which slightly affects nuclear size. More neutrons → slightly larger nucleus → marginally larger atomic radius (due to increased nuclear volume). This effect is tiny (picometer scale) compared to electron cloud differences between elements. For most purposes, isotopes are considered to have the same atomic radius. The 'nuclear volume effect' (also called isotope shift) is observable in precision spectroscopy.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of basicity of oxides?
options: CaO > MgO > BeO > Al₂O₃
Al₂O₃ > MgO > CaO > BeO
BeO > MgO > CaO > Al₂O₃
MgO > CaO > Al₂O₃ > BeO
explanation: Basicity of oxides correlates with metallic character/electropositivity of the metal (Group 2 down the group, metallic character increases): CaO is more basic than MgO (Ca is more electropositive than Mg). BeO is amphoteric (Be has very high charge/radius ratio, resembles Al due to diagonal relationship). Al₂O₃ is also amphoteric. Therefore, basicity order: CaO (strongly basic) > MgO (moderately basic) > BeO (amphoteric, weakly basic) > Al₂O₃ (amphoteric). So CaO > MgO > BeO > Al₂O₃ matches option A. Going down Group 2, oxides become more basic because larger cations have lower charge density.

question: The element with Z=21 (Scandium) has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d¹ 4s². Sc belongs to which block and has how many unpaired electrons?
options: s-block, 0 unpaired electrons
d-block, 1 unpaired electron
p-block, 1 unpaired electron
f-block, 0 unpaired electrons
explanation: Sc (Z=21): [Ar] 3d¹ 4s². The last electron enters the 3d subshell → d-block element (first transition metal). The 3d¹ has one electron in one of the five degenerate 3d orbitals → 1 unpaired electron → paramagnetic. Sc is in Group 3 (3d¹ 4s² → 1+2=3 valence electrons in d+s). It's the first d-block element in Period 4. The common oxidation state is +3 (loses 3d¹ + 4s²). Scandium oxide Sc₂O₃ is used in high-intensity metal halide lamps and as scandium-aluminum alloy for aerospace applications.

question: A NEET-pattern question: An atom X has first ionization energy of 520 kJ/mol and second ionization energy of 7298 kJ/mol. The element X is most likely:
options: A halogen in Group 17
An alkaline earth metal in Group 2
An alkali metal in Group 1
A transition metal in Group 6
explanation: The dramatic jump between IE₁ (520 kJ/mol) and IE₂ (7298 kJ/mol) — a factor of ~14 — indicates that removing the 2nd electron disrupts a very stable electron configuration (noble gas core). This occurs for Group 1 elements (alkali metals): 1st ionization removes the lone ns¹ electron (relatively easy), but the 2nd electron must be removed from the noble gas core (extremely hard). IE₁ = 520 kJ/mol closely matches sodium (Na, IE₁ = 496 kJ/mol) or lithium (Li, IE₁ = 520 kJ/mol — this matches Li exactly!). Li is indeed an alkali metal. The IE₂/IE₁ ratio is the key — Group 1 shows the greatest jump at IE₁→IE₂.

question: Which statement about the periodic table is INCORRECT?
options: Period number equals the number of the outermost electron shell
Group number for main group elements equals the number of valence electrons
Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties due to the same number of valence electrons
All transition metals are placed in the f-block of the periodic table
explanation: Option D is INCORRECT: Transition metals are d-block elements (groups 3-12), NOT f-block. f-block elements are the lanthanides (4f filling) and actinides (5f filling), placed separately below the main table. Options A, B, C are all correct statements: Period number = principal quantum number of outermost shell ✓. For s and p block (main group), group number = valence electrons ✓. Same group → same valence electron configuration → similar chemical behavior ✓. The confusion between d-block and f-block is a common error source in competitive exams.

question: The graph of atomic radius vs. atomic number shows a periodic pattern with peaks at which elements?
options: Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe)
Alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs)
Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I)
Transition metals (Sc, Ti, V, ...)
explanation: In the graph of atomic radius vs. atomic number: PEAKS occur at alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs) — they have the largest radii in their period (leftmost, lowest Zeff, one electron in a new outer shell). VALLEYS occur at halogens (smallest in their period) and even smaller at noble gases in terms of covalent radius. The graph shows a saw-tooth pattern: sharp increase from noble gas to alkali metal (new electron shell added), then gradual decrease across the period (increasing Zeff). The periodicity of this graph visually demonstrates the periodic law.

question: The shielding effectiveness of electrons follows the order:
options: s > p > d > f
f > d > p > s
p > s > d > f
s > d > p > f
explanation: Shielding (screening) effectiveness of subshells: s electrons have the highest probability of being found close to the nucleus (greatest penetration), so they shield outer electrons most effectively. Penetration and shielding order: s > p > d > f. This is because s orbitals are spherical with highest electron density near nucleus; p orbitals have a nodal plane at nucleus; d orbitals have less penetration; f orbitals have negligible penetration near nucleus. This order directly explains: (1) why 4f electrons in lanthanides shield poorly (explaining lanthanide contraction), (2) Slater's rules for calculating shielding constants (σ).

question: Why does fluorine not exhibit positive oxidation states unlike other halogens (Cl, Br, I)?
options: Fluorine has too few electrons to donate
Fluorine lacks available d orbitals in its valence shell (n=2) to accommodate expanded octets or positive oxidation states
Fluorine has very low ionization energy making it hard to achieve positive oxidation
Fluorine cannot form more than one bond
explanation: Fluorine (n=2) has no d orbitals in its valence shell. Positive oxidation states in halogens require expansion beyond the octet — e.g., Cl in ClF₃ (+3), ClF₅ (+5), and in perchlorate (+7). This expansion is possible because Cl, Br, and I have available 3d, 4d, and 5d orbitals respectively to accommodate additional bonds. Fluorine, limited to n=2 shell (only 2s and 2p available), CANNOT expand its octet, so it cannot achieve positive oxidation states. Additionally, F is the most electronegative element — it will never give electrons to another atom. Hence F shows only −1 and 0 oxidation states.

question: CASE-BASED: A researcher is studying elements in Period 4 and notes that the element at Z=29 (copper) has an unexpectedly high second ionization energy compared to elements on either side of it in the periodic table. Why?
options: Copper has a very large atomic radius making it hard to remove a second electron
After losing one electron (from 4s), copper achieves a completely filled 3d¹⁰ configuration, which is especially stable, making removal of a second electron much more difficult
Copper's nuclear charge is too low to hold its second electron properly
Copper forms strong metallic bonds that resist ionization
explanation: Copper (Z=29) ground state: [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ (due to extra stability of full d). Cu⁺ = [Ar] 3d¹⁰ — a completely filled 3d subshell. This full d¹⁰ configuration is extra stable (symmetric, maximum exchange energy). To form Cu²⁺, the 2nd electron must be removed from this stable 3d¹⁰ shell, requiring significantly more energy than expected. This is why Cu²⁺ is stable but Cu⁺ → Cu²⁺ requires more energy than for neighboring Zn or Ni. This also explains why Cu²⁺ (blue) is more common than Cu⁺ in aqueous chemistry despite Cu⁺ having a lower charge. The unexpected IE₂ peak at Cu is a classic periodic trend question.

question: The screening constants (σ) for a 4s electron in potassium according to Slater's rules is approximately:
explanation: For K (Z=19, configuration [Ar] 4s¹): The 4s electron is shielded by all inner electrons. Slater's rules: electrons in the same group (4s) contribute 0.35; electrons in the (n-1) shell (n=3) contribute 0.85 each; electrons in (n-2) and lower contribute 1.00 each. K configuration in Slater notation: (1s²)(2s²2p⁶)(3s²3p⁶)(3d⁰)(4s¹). Shielding for 4s: n-1 shells (n=3: 8 electrons at 0.85): 8 × 0.85 = 6.8; n≤2 shells (1s², 2s², 2p⁶ = 10 electrons at 1.00): 10 × 1.00 = 10; same shell: 0. σ = 6.8 + 10 = 16.8 ≈ 17. Zeff = 19 - 17 = 2. The outer electron of K experiences a Zeff of about 2, explaining its relatively easy removal.
